title: Selection of one or more item

concepts.title: Selection of one or more different items
concepts.conceptBody:
Selection from Different Items

The number of ways of selecting one or more items (At least one) from a group of $\text{n}$ distinct items is ${{2}^{\text{n}}}-1.$.

Logic: For at least one, the selections can be done in the following manner.

${{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}+{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}+{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}\ldots {{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}$

But we know that ${{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{0}}+{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}+{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}\ldots {{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}=2\text{n}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}+{{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}\ldots {{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{n}}}={{2}^{\text{n}}}-1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ {removing the case of no selection}

Hence the answer

Q) You want to invite 5 of your friends to your party. In how many ways can you invite atleast one of them. 

Solution: 

They all can or may be some of them turn up for your party. But atleast one of them turns up. So, every person has 2 options, one to come to party, the other being not turning up for party. So accordingly, to the previous formula the answer is ${{2}^{5}}-1=31$ ways. Subtracting the case when all of them do not arrive for the party.

concepts.title: Selection of one or more identical items
concepts.conceptBody:
Selection from Identical Items

(a) The number of ways of selecting ' $r$ ' items out of $n$ identical items are 1.

Logic: There is no meaning of arrangement & selection in identical items.

for ex. take 3 identical black balls & 3 different black balls ${{\text{B}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{B}}_{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{B}}_{3}}$

You can see, selecting 2 out of 3 results in only 1 case, whereas there are 3 ways for different balls.

(b) The total number of ways of selecting zero or more i.e. atleast one item from a group of $\text{n}$ identical items are $\text{n}+1$

Logic: From case a, we know that selecting any number of items from $\text{n}$ identical can be done in 1 way only. So,

Selecting none $=1$

1 item $=1$

2 items $=1$

$\text{n}$ items $=1$

adding all, total ways $=\text{n}+1$

(c) The total number of ways of selecting some or all out of $p+q+r$ items where $p$ are alike of one kind, $q$ are alike of second kind and rest are alike of third kind is $[\left( p+1 \right)\left( q+1 \right)\left( r+1 \right]-1$

Logic: Again from the previous case, if

we select $0,1,2\ldots \text{p}$ items $\to \left( \text{p}+1 \right)$ ways

$0,1,2\ldots $ q items $\to \left( q+1 \right)$ ways

$0,1,2\ldots \text{r}$ items $\to \left( \text{r}+1 \right)$ ways

Since any of them can be selected, separately or in combination.

$\therefore $ Total ways $=\left( \text{p}+1 \right)\left( \text{q}+1 \right)\left( \text{r}+1 \right)$

Subtract 1, so as to remove the case of no selection i.e. 0 items of type p, 0 items of type q & r. Note this will be just one case not three.

Q) Find the total number of factors of 7875 

(a) including 1 

(b) excluding 1 i.e. proper factors 

Solution: 

We can write $7875={{3}^{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{5}^{3}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{7}^{1}}$

Now this question is analogous to previous case as we can consider 3 groups of identical items

as ' 3 ' group having 2 identical 3's

'5' group having 3 identical 5's

'7' group having 1 identical 7's

Now solving the parts

(a) including 1

for 1 to be one of the factors, it should be $\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 3 & 5 & 7 \\ \end{array}$

i.e. selecting 0 from each group.

And hence answer is

$\left( 2+1 \right)\left( 3+1 \right)\left( 1+1 \right)=24$ factors

( Note: No ' -1 ' as we have to take the case when none is selected, which we initially subtracted from the formula)

(b) excluding $1/$ proper factors

Now we just have to remove the case when 1 is also the factor which only in 1case

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Total ways $=24-1=23$ factors

concepts.title: Selection of one or more items from a group containing both identical and different items
concepts.conceptBody:
Selection from a group containing both identical and different items

The number of ways of selecting one or more items from $p$ identical of one kind, q identical items of second kind, similarly for $\text{r}$ and $\text{n}$ different items is

$\left( p+1 \right)\left( q+1 \right)\left( r+1 \right){{2}^{n}}-1$

Logic: We can come to the answer by using the results derived earlier. $p,q,r$ can be selected in $\left( p+1 \right),\left( q+1 \right)\And \left( r+1 \right)$ ways, as done earlier. Now for distinct elements there are only 2 options for them, if either they are selected or rejected. So, for $n$ such distinct terms there will be 2 2 2…. (n terms) ways of selecting one of them.

Combining both identical & distinct elements

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Total ways $=\left( \text{p}+1 \right)\left( \text{q}+1 \right)\left( \text{r}+1 \right){{2}^{\text{n}}}$

Subtract 1 to remove the case of no selection.

Q) Let a number be represented by ${{a}^{3}}{{b}^{4}}{{c}^{5}} def$ where $a,b,c,d,e,f\in $ prime numbers. Find the number of factors of such a number. 

Solution: 

Here in this question there are 3 identical group of a's, b's & c's. Whereas d, e & f are distinct elements.

Group of a having 3 a’s, b 4 b’s, c 5 c’s

$\therefore \quad$ Total factors $=(3+1)(4+1)(5+1) 222$

$=960$ factors

If we exclude factor 1 then there are 959 factors.

concepts.title: Finding Number of Divisors of a Given Natural Number
concepts.conceptBody:
Divisors of a given natural number 

Let $\text{n}\in \text{N}$ and $\text{n}=\text{p}_{1}^{{{\alpha }_{1}}}\cdot \text{p}_{2}^{{{\alpha }_{2}}}\cdot \text{p}_{3}^{{{\alpha }_{3}}}\ldots ..\text{p}_{\text{k}}^{\alpha \text{k}}$, where ${{\text{p}}_{1}},{{\text{p}}_{2}},{{\text{p}}_{3}},\ldots {{\text{p}}_{\text{n}}}$ are different prime numbers and ${{\alpha }_{1}},{{\alpha }_{2}},{{\alpha }_{3}},\ldots {{\alpha }_{\text{k}}}$ are natural numbers then : - $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

  • the total numbers of divisors of $\text{N}$ including 1 and $\text{n}$ is

$=\left( {{\alpha }_{1}}+1 \right)\left( {{\alpha }_{2}}+1 \right)\left( {{\alpha }_{3}}+1 \right)\ldots \left( {{\alpha }_{k}}+1 \right)$

  • the total number of divisors of $n$ excluding 1 and $n$ is

$=\left( {{\alpha }_{1}}+1 \right)\left( {{\alpha }_{2}}+1 \right)\left( {{\alpha }_{3}}+1 \right)\ldots \left( {{\alpha }_{k}}+1 \right)-1$

  • the sum of these divisors is

$=\left( p_{1}^{0}+p_{1}^{1}+p_{1}^{2}+\ldots .+p_{1}^{{{\alpha }_{1}}} \right)\left( p_{2}^{0}+p_{2}^{1}+p_{2}^{2}+\ldots .+p_{1}^{\alpha } \right)\ldots ..\left( p_{k}^{0}+p_{k}^{1}+p_{k}^{2}+\ldots .+p_{k}^{{{\alpha }_{1}}} \right)$

(Use sum of G.P. in each bracket)

  • $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ the number of ways in which $\text{n}$ can be resolved as a product of two factors is

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \frac{1}{2}\left( {{\alpha }_{1}}+1 \right)\left( {{\alpha }_{2}}+1 \right)\ldots \left( {{a}_{\text{k}}}+1 \right),\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ if }\!\!~\!\!\text{ n }\!\!~\!\!\text{ is }\!\!~\!\!\text{ not }\!\!~\!\!\text{ a }\!\!~\!\!\text{ perfect }\!\!~\!\!\text{ square }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ {} & \left[ \frac{1}{2}\left( {{\alpha }_{1}}+1 \right)\left( {{\alpha }_{2}}+1 \right)\ldots \left( {{a}_{\text{k}}}+1 \right) \right],\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ if }\!\!~\!\!\text{ n }\!\!~\!\!\text{ is }\!\!~\!\!\text{ a }\!\!~\!\!\text{ perfect }\!\!~\!\!\text{ square }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \end{array}$

  • the number of ways in which a composite number $\text{n}$ can be resolved into two factors which are relatively prime (or coprime) to each other is equal to ${{2}^{\text{k}-1}}$ where $\text{k}$ is the number of different factors (or different primes) in $\text{n}$.

Q) If n = 10800, then find the 

(a) total number of divisors of n 

(b) the number of even divisors 

(c) the number of divisors of the form 4m+2 

(d) the number of divisors which are multiples of 15 

Solution: 

$\text{n}=10800={{2}^{4}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{3}^{3}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{5}^{2}}$

Any divisor of $\text{n}$ will be of the form ${{2}^{\text{a}}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{3}^{\text{b}}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{5}^{\text{c}}}$ where $0\le \text{a}<4,0\le \text{b}3,0\le \text{c}\le 2$

For any distinct choices of $a,b$ and $c$, we get a divisor of $n$

(a) total number of divisors $=\left( 4+1 \right)\left( 3+1 \right)\left( 2+1 \right)=60$

(b) for a divisor to be even, a should be at least one. So total number of even divisors

$=4\left( 3+1 \right)\left( 2+1 \right)=48\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left[ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ no }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left( 4+1 \right) \right]$

(c) $4\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}+2=2\left( 2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}+1 \right)$. In any divisor of the form $4\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}+2$, a should be exactly 1 . So number of divisors of the form $4\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}+2=1\left( 3+1 \right)\left( 2+1 \right)=12$

(d) A divisor of $\text{n}$ will be a multiple of 15 if $\text{b}$ is at least one and $\text{c}$ is at least one. So number of such divisors $=\left( 4+1 \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }3\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }2=30$

Q) Find the number of divisors of 428652000 excluding the number and unity. Find also the sum of the divisors. 

$428652000={{2}^{5}}\cdot {{3}^{7}}\cdot {{5}^{3}}\cdot {{7}^{2}}$ 

Solution: 

Any divisor of the given number has to be a combination of the 2's (five); 3's (seven); 5's (three) and 7's (two).

There are $5+1=6$ ways of selecting none or one or two etc., or all the 2's. Similar argument repeats for the other numbers.

$\therefore $ The number of divisors $=\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 6 & 8 & 4 & 3 \\ \end{array}=576$

This includes 1 and the given number also.

Excluding these two, the number of divisors 574

With regard to the sum of the divisors

Any divisor is of the form ${{2}^{\text{p}}}{{3}^{\text{q}}}{{5}^{\text{s}}}{{7}^{\text{t}}}$ where $0\le \text{p}\le 5;0\le \text{q}\le 7;0\le \text{s}\le 3$ and $0\le \text{t}\le 2$

Thus, the sum of the divisors is

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \left( 1+2+\ldots +{{2}^{5}} \right)\left( 1+3+\ldots +{{3}^{7}} \right)\left( 1+5+\ldots +{{5}^{3}} \right)\left( 1-7+{{7}^{2}} \right) \\ {} & ~=\left( {{2}^{6}}-1 \right)\left( {{3}^{8}}-1 \right)\left( {{5}^{4}}-1 \right)\left( {{7}^{3}}-1 \right)/48 \\ \end{array}$

examples.title: In how many ways can 10 true-false questions be replied
A man has 7 friends. In how many ways he can invite one or more of them for a tea party
Out of 10 white, 9 black and 7 red balls, the number of ways in which selection of one or more balls can be made, is
Out of 6 books, in how many ways can a set of one or more books be chosen
In how many ways can 6 persons be selected from 4 officers and 8 constables, if at least one officer is to be included
A man has 10 friends. In how many ways he can invite one or more of them to a party
